food_subgroup: Thinly sliced beef sirloin marinated in soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, garlic, ginger, and Asian pear, then grilled

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a grilled beef category (FOODON_03315604) but no specific concept for Korean marinated bulgogi. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Thinly sliced marinated Korean grilled beef (bulgogi) — soy sauce, sesame oil, Asian pear, sugar, garlic, ginger marinade
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00MJ covers beef meat products; no specific bulgogi facet exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for bulgogi or Korean marinated grilled beef as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of bulgogi is approximately 200g of grilled marinated beef, typically served with steamed rice and banchan side dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, sirloin, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, broiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with soy sauce and sesame oil marinade component data from USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked beef sirloin (lean, broiled) as the primary base, with marinade components (soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, Asian pear) incorporated proportionally to reflect the finished bulgogi dish. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with published Korean food composition data for bulgogi (NIAS Korea / FAO/INFOODS SEA Table cross-referenced). The NIAS/FAO source is not on the approved list; USDA FoodData Central is used as the primary approved source with values cross-validated against NIAS data. Values are plausible and internally consistent.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Beef Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for Korean marinated grilled beef based on high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate meat dish profile
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for bulgogi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 42 (low) is estimated based on: (1) high protein content (20.5g/100g) substantially suppressing glycaemic response; (2) moderate fat (8.5g/100g) slowing gastric emptying; (3) carbohydrate (8.5g/100g) primarily from marinade sugars including fructose from Asian pear, which has a lower GI than glucose; (4) analogous high-protein marinated meat dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database cluster in the 40–50 range. GL per 200g serving = (42 × 8.5 × 2) / 100 = 7.14, rounded to 7.1. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (548mg/100g) from soy sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: A standard 200g restaurant serving delivers approximately 1,096mg sodium — exceeding 50% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000mg from this dish alone; patients with hypertension should limit portions to 100–150g, request reduced soy sauce in the marinade, and account for additional sodium from rice and banchan side dishes. The potassium content (320mg/100g) provides partial compensatory benefit for blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic load (GL 7.1 per 200g serving) with high protein and moderate fat attenuating glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bulgogi is suitable for most people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions: the low GL of 7.1 per serving, combined with 20.5g protein and 8.5g fat per 100g, substantially blunts postprandial glucose rise; zinc (4.5mg/100g) and magnesium (28mg/100g) additionally support insulin signalling, though the marinade sugar content (6.5g/100g) should be noted when calculating total carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (548mg/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is moderate at 2.8g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the high sodium content (548mg/100g) is the dominant concern and warrants portion control and marinade modification; saturated fat is moderate (2.8g/100g, well below fatty beef cuts), and the lean sirloin base provides beneficial zinc (4.5mg/100g) and selenium (25mcg/100g) which support antioxidant defence, making bulgogi a preferable choice over higher-fat Korean beef dishes when sodium is managed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory marinade components (ginger, garlic) and high zinc and selenium content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bulgogi is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients: ginger (gingerols, shogaols) and garlic (allicin) in the marinade have documented anti-inflammatory properties; zinc (4.5mg/100g) supports immune modulation; selenium (25mcg/100g) supports glutathione peroxidase antioxidant defence; and the moderate saturated fat level (2.8g/100g) is lower than fattier beef cuts, reducing pro-inflammatory lipid load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bulgogi (불고기, literally 'fire meat' — bul = fire, gogi = meat) is one of Korea's oldest and most internationally celebrated dishes, with origins traced to the Goguryeo era. It consists of thinly sliced beef sirloin or ribeye marinated in a carefully balanced sauce of soy sauce (umami and salt), sesame oil (nuttiness), sugar and Asian pear (sweetness and enzymatic tenderisation — the bromelain and actinidin enzymes in Asian pear break down muscle proteins for a silky, butter-tender texture), garlic and ginger (aromatic depth), and black pepper. After marinating for several hours, the beef is grilled over high heat — the natural sugars caramelise into a glossy, slightly charred exterior while the interior remains juicy and tender. The result is a perfect harmony of sweet, savoury, and lightly smoky flavours. Nutritionally, bulgogi is an excellent lean protein source (20.5g/100g), with outstanding zinc (4.5mg/100g) and selenium (25mcg/100g) from the beef sirloin base. The high sodium content (548mg/100g) from soy sauce is the primary clinical concern, particularly for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The glycaemic load is low (GL 7.1 per 200g serving), making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management. Bulgogi is typically eaten with steamed rice, wrapped in lettuce leaves (ssam), or as part of a multi-dish Korean meal with banchan side dishes. It is a staple of Korean BBQ restaurants worldwide and a flagship dish of Korean cuisine.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are internally consistent and cross-validated against NIAS Korea / FAO/INFOODS SEA data, but the primary approved source used is USDA FoodData Central (adapted from cooked beef sirloin with marinade components), not an exact bulgogi entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the finished bulgogi dish (including marinade contribution from sesame oil) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the finished grilled bulgogi dish is not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for lean beef sirloin per USDA FoodData Central. The input source cited NIAS Korea Food Composition Database and FAO/INFOODS SEA Table; these are not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. USDA FoodData Central has been used as the primary approved source, with values cross-validated against the NIAS/FAO data; all values are consistent. GI of 42 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database framework for high-protein marinated meat dishes; no direct bulgogi GI measurement is available in the approved GI database. GL calculated as (42 × 8.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 7.14, rounded to 7.1 for a 200g serving.
